description: Quantifies how independent the EU/EFTA could become from US-controlled software, per application sector, if digital-sovereignty policy is taken seriously after Trump v. Slaughter (2026). Independence is modelled on two separate axes: license sovereignty (is the code/model freely licensed and forkable, hence not US-switchable) and governance sovereignty (do stewardship, roadmap and funding sit in EU/EFTA hands). Current shares carry 'measured value' with a source; 3- and 5-year min/likely/max projections and per-year market sizes carry 'assumed value' with a numeric confidence (0-1) so weak assumptions surface later. All content in English per project convention. Sector share = realistically-expectable share of non-US-controlled solutions (actual substitution reading, not mere technical replaceability).

words.name: software
words.description: a set of computer programs and associated documentation and data.

words.name: sovereignty
words.description: the defining authority within an individual consciousness, social construct, or territory.

words.name: digital
words.description: relating to data represented as discrete numerical values.

words.name: license
words.description: an official permission or permit to do, use, or own something.

words.name: governance
words.description: the way that organizations or countries are managed at the highest level, and the systems for doing this.

words.name: EU
words.description: the European Union, a supranational political and economic union of member states located primarily in Europe.

words.name: US
words.description: the United States of America, a country primarily located in North America.

words.name: control
words.description: the power to influence or direct people's behaviour or the course of events.

words.name: share
words.description: a part or portion of a larger amount which is divided among a number of people, or to which a number of people contribute.

words.name: percent
words.description: a number or ratio expressed as a fraction of 100.

words.name: market
words.description: a composition of systems, institutions, procedures, social relations or infrastructures whereby parties engage in exchange.

words.name: size
words.description: the relative extent of something; a thing's overall dimensions or magnitude.

words.name: euro
words.description: the official currency of the eurozone.

words.name: billion
words.description: the natural number following 999,999,999 and preceding 1,000,000,001; the short-scale 10^9.

words.name: year
words.description: the orbital period of a planetary body, in particular Earth's about the Sun.
the current calendar year of the measured shares.
the 3-year projection horizon (2026 + 3 years).
the 5-year projection horizon (2026 + 5 years).

words.name: minimum
words.description: the least or smallest amount possible, attainable, or required.

words.name: likely
words.description: the outcome carrying the highest probability mass under the assumed scenario.

words.name: maximum
words.description: the greatest or largest amount possible or attainable.

words.name: measured
words.description: ascertained by an external source rather than assumed; provenance qualifier marking a stored number as sourced.

words.name: assumed
words.description: estimated or projected rather than sourced; provenance qualifier marking a stored number as a conjecture carrying a confidence.

words.name: value
words.description: a stored number in the graph; combined with 'measured'/'assumed' to mark its provenance.

words.name: confidence
words.description: degree of belief, 0 to 1, that an assumed value is correct; low confidence flags an assumption as worth checking first.

words.name: open-source
words.description: software released under a license in which the copyright holder grants users the rights to use, study, change, and distribute the software and its source code.

words.name: forkable
words.description: able to be copied into an independent codebase that can be developed separately; the property that makes freely-licensed software non-switchable by any single party.

words.name: stewardship
words.description: the job of supervising or taking care of something, such as an organization or property.

words.name: operating
words.description: of or relating to the system software that manages computer hardware and software resources.

words.name: system
words.description: a group of interacting or interrelated elements that act according to a set of rules to form a unified whole.

words.name: desktop
words.description: a personal computer designed for regular use at a single location on or near a desk.

words.name: server
words.description: computer hardware or software that provides functionality for other programs or devices, called clients.

words.name: smartphone
words.description: a portable computer device that combines mobile telephone functions and computing functions into one unit.

words.name: office
words.description: application software designed for creating, editing and managing documents, spreadsheets and presentations.

words.name: browser
words.description: an application for accessing websites.

words.name: engine
words.description: a core software component that performs a fundamental function for other software; here a browser layout engine.

words.name: cloud
words.description: on-demand availability of computer system resources, especially data storage and computing power, without direct active management by the user.

words.name: collaboration
words.description: the action of working with someone to produce or create something; here groupware/file-sharing platforms.

words.name: messaging
words.description: the exchange of messages between people, here including social and instant messaging.

words.name: videoconference
words.description: the conduct of a conference between two or more participants at different sites by using computer networks to transmit audio and video data.

words.name: email
words.description: messages distributed by electronic means from one computer user to one or more recipients via a network.

words.name: groupware
words.description: software designed to help people working on a common task to attain their goals; here the mail/calendar server backend.

words.name: ERP
words.description: enterprise resource planning: integrated management of main business processes, often in real time and mediated by software.

words.name: education
words.description: the transmission of knowledge, skills, and character traits; here learning-management software.

words.name: map
words.description: a symbolic depiction emphasizing relationships between elements of some space; here mapping and navigation software.

words.name: password
words.description: a secret word or string of characters used for user authentication; here password-management software.

words.name: database
words.description: an organized collection of data stored and accessed electronically.

words.name: backup
words.description: a copy of computer data taken and stored elsewhere so that it may be used to restore the original after a data-loss event; here backup/storage software.

words.name: container
words.description: operating-system-level virtualization that packages software with its dependencies; here container and orchestration software.

words.name: virtualization
words.description: the act of creating a virtual version of computer hardware platforms, storage devices, and network resources.

words.name: proxy
words.description: a server application that acts as an intermediary between a client and a destination server; here web/reverse-proxy software.

words.name: DNS
words.description: the Domain Name System, a hierarchical naming system for computers and services connected to the internet.

words.name: firewall
words.description: a network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predetermined security rules.

words.name: identity
words.description: the management of individual identities, their authentication and authorization within or across systems; here identity/SSO software.

words.name: forge
words.description: a web-based collaborative software platform for developing and sharing code; here CI/CD and code hosting.

words.name: monitoring
words.description: the observation of a system's state and performance over time; here observability software.

words.name: LLM
words.description: a large language model: a type of machine-learning model designed for natural-language processing tasks.

words.name: framework
words.description: an abstraction providing generic functionality that can be selectively changed by additional user-written code; here a machine-learning framework.

words.name: machine-learning
words.description: the study of computer algorithms that can improve automatically through experience and by the use of data.

words.name: inferencing
words.description: the process of running a trained machine-learning model to generate outputs, here run locally rather than via a remote API.

words.name: vector
words.description: here a vector database storing embeddings for similarity search, used in retrieval-augmented generation.

words.name: search
words.description: a software system designed to carry out web searches.

words.name: Debian
words.description: a free and open-source Linux distribution developed by the community-supported Debian Project.

words.name: Linux
words.description: a family of open-source Unix-like operating systems based on the Linux kernel.

words.name: LineageOS
words.description: a free and open-source operating system for smartphones and tablets, based on the Android mobile platform.

words.name: GrapheneOS
words.description: a privacy- and security-focused mobile operating system for selected Pixel devices, compatible with Android apps.

words.name: LibreOffice
words.description: a free and open-source office productivity software suite.

words.name: Firefox
words.description: a free and open-source web browser developed by the Mozilla Foundation.

words.name: Gecko
words.description: a browser engine developed by Mozilla; one of only two non-Chinese engines not derived from Chromium.

words.name: Nextcloud
words.description: a suite of client-server software for creating and using file-hosting services; German-led, central to the Gaia-X European cloud effort.

words.name: Matrix
words.description: an open standard and communication protocol for real-time communication; basis of the Element client used by the German Bundeswehr.

words.name: Mastodon
words.description: free and open-source software for running self-hosted social networking services, using the ActivityPub protocol.

words.name: Jitsi
words.description: a collection of free and open-source multiplatform voice, video conferencing and instant messaging applications.

words.name: Odoo
words.description: a Belgian suite of business management software tools including ERP, CRM and others; dual-licensed.

words.name: SAP
words.description: a German multinational software company; the largest European enterprise-software vendor, proprietary and non-US-controlled.

words.name: Moodle
words.description: a free and open-source learning management system.

words.name: OpenStreetMap
words.description: a free, open geographic database updated and maintained by a community of volunteers.

words.name: KeePassXC
words.description: a free and open-source password manager, a community fork of KeePass.

words.name: PostgreSQL
words.description: a free and open-source relational database management system emphasizing extensibility and SQL compliance.

words.name: Proxmox
words.description: an Austrian open-source server virtualization management platform based on Debian, KVM and LXC.

words.name: VMware
words.description: a US virtualization and cloud computing vendor, acquired by Broadcom in 2023; its post-acquisition pricing drove a migration wave.

words.name: Kubernetes
words.description: an open-source container-orchestration system; freely licensed but governed by the US-based Cloud Native Computing Foundation.

words.name: nginx
words.description: a web server that can also be used as a reverse proxy, load balancer, mail proxy and HTTP cache.

words.name: Knot
words.description: an open-source DNS server developed by CZ.NIC, the Czech domain registry.

words.name: OPNsense
words.description: an open-source, FreeBSD-based firewall and routing platform.

words.name: Keycloak
words.description: an open-source identity and access management solution; freely licensed but stewarded by US-based Red Hat.

words.name: Forgejo
words.description: a self-hosted lightweight software forge, a community fork of Gitea, hosted by the European Codeberg e.V.

words.name: Prometheus
words.description: a free software application used for event monitoring and alerting, a graduated CNCF project.

words.name: Apertus
words.description: a fully open, multilingual large language model released in 2025 by EPFL, ETH Zurich and CSCS; open weights, open data and open training process under Apache 2.0.

words.name: Mistral
words.description: a French artificial-intelligence company producing open-weight large language models.

words.name: PyTorch
words.description: a machine-learning framework based on the Torch library; freely licensed (BSD) but governed by the US-based PyTorch Foundation under the Linux Foundation, originating at Meta.

words.name: Qdrant
words.description: an open-source vector database and similarity-search engine; the company is Berlin-based.

words.name: YaCy
words.description: a free distributed search engine built on the principles of peer-to-peer networks.

words.name: Trump
words.description: Donald John Trump, 47th president of the United States.

words.name: Slaughter
words.description: Rebecca Kelly Slaughter, a commissioner of the US Federal Trade Commission whose removal by the President was upheld.

words.name: FTC
words.description: the United States Federal Trade Commission, the enforcement authority the EU adequacy decision relies upon.

words.name: independence
words.description: the quality of not being controlled by another; here the EU-law requirement of an independent data-protection oversight authority.

words.name: convergence
words.description: the property of a process settling toward a fixed point that needs no further development; here applied to a data format/program reaching maintenance-only stability.

words.name: community
words.description: a social unit with commonality such as norms, religion, values, customs, or identity; here the developer community behind a software project.

triples.name: software sovereignty
triples.description: the degree to which control over a piece of software's existence, evolution and operation rests outside US jurisdiction.

triples.name: license sovereignty
triples.description: axis 1: whether the code/model is freely licensed and forkable, hence cannot be switched off by any single (US) party. Near-100% for any open-source artefact regardless of origin.

triples.name: governance sovereignty
triples.description: axis 2: whether stewardship, roadmap and funding sit in EU/EFTA hands. Can be near-zero even when license sovereignty is full (e.g. PyTorch, Gecko, Kubernetes, Keycloak).

triples.name: non-US control
triples.description: the property of a solution not being directable by US jurisdiction; the quantity the sector shares measure.

triples.name: market share
triples.description: the realistically-expectable share of non-US-controlled solutions within a sector's EU/EFTA market.

triples.name: market size
triples.description: the approximate annual EU/EFTA market volume of a sector.

triples.name: euro billion
triples.description: monetary measure: billions of euro per year.

triples.name: measured value
triples.description: provenance qualifier: the number is sourced from an external reference, not assumed.

triples.name: assumed value
triples.description: provenance qualifier: the number is a projection/estimate carrying a confidence, not sourced.

triples.name: desktop operating system
triples.description: the OS sector for desktop and laptop computers (Debian/Linux vs Windows/macOS).

triples.name: server operating system
triples.description: the OS sector for servers (Linux vs Windows Server), where Linux is already the industry default.

triples.name: smartphone operating system
triples.description: the mobile OS sector (LineageOS/GrapheneOS vs Android/iOS).

triples.name: office software
triples.description: the office-suite sector (LibreOffice vs Microsoft 365).

triples.name: browser software
triples.description: the web-browser sector (Firefox vs Chrome/Edge/Safari).

triples.name: browser engine
triples.description: the underlying layout-engine sector; only Gecko (Mozilla) and Chromium (Google) remain among non-Chinese engines.

triples.name: cloud collaboration
triples.description: the collaboration/file-sharing frontend sector (Nextcloud vs Google Workspace/Microsoft 365); distinct from the hyperscale IaaS layer beneath it.

triples.name: messaging software
triples.description: the social/instant-messaging sector (Matrix, Mastodon vs US platforms).

triples.name: videoconference software
triples.description: the video-conferencing sector (Jitsi, BigBlueButton vs Zoom/Teams).

triples.name: email groupware
triples.description: the mail/calendar server-backend sector (Open-Xchange, SOGo vs Microsoft Exchange).

triples.name: education software
triples.description: the learning-management sector (Moodle, ILIAS), where FOSS is already strong in the EU.

triples.name: map software
triples.description: the mapping/navigation sector (OpenStreetMap, Organic Maps vs Google Maps).

triples.name: password software
triples.description: the password-management sector (KeePassXC vs proprietary managers).

triples.name: database software
triples.description: the database sector (PostgreSQL, MariaDB), where FOSS is already a strong default.

triples.name: backup software
triples.description: the backup/storage sector (Ceph, Restic, Borg).

triples.name: container orchestration
triples.description: the container/orchestration sector (Kubernetes, Podman); license-sovereign but US-governed (CNCF).

triples.name: virtualization software
triples.description: the virtualization sector (Proxmox, KVM vs VMware), in an unusual EU-favourable migration window after the Broadcom price shock.

triples.name: proxy software
triples.description: the web/reverse-proxy sector (nginx, Caddy, HAProxy), where the EU is technically strong.

triples.name: DNS software
triples.description: the DNS/network-services sector (Knot, PowerDNS, Unbound, BIND).

triples.name: firewall software
triples.description: the firewall/security sector (OPNsense, pfSense, nftables).

triples.name: identity software
triples.description: the identity/SSO sector (Keycloak, Authentik, FreeIPA), a key lever for migrating off Microsoft Entra.

triples.name: forge software
triples.description: the CI/CD and code-hosting sector (Forgejo/Codeberg vs GitHub/GitLab).

triples.name: monitoring software
triples.description: the monitoring/observability sector (Prometheus, Zabbix, Checkmk).

triples.name: LLM software
triples.description: the large-language-model sector (Apertus, Mistral, open models vs OpenAI/Anthropic/Google).

triples.name: machine-learning framework
triples.description: the ML-framework sector (PyTorch, TensorFlow): freely licensed but with almost no EU-governed alternative.

triples.name: local inferencing
triples.description: the local-inference sector (llama.cpp, Ollama, vLLM) running models on own infrastructure.

triples.name: vector database
triples.description: the vector-DB/RAG sector (Qdrant [DE], Weaviate [NL] vs Pinecone), one of the few EU-competitive AI subfields.

triples.name: search software
triples.description: the web-search sector (YaCy, OpenWebSearch.eu); the hardest index-scale lock-in, even DuckDuckGo/Ecosia ride foreign indexes.

triples.name: Trump v. Slaughter
triples.description: the 2026 US Supreme Court decision holding the FTC's for-cause removal protection unconstitutional, removing the FTC's structural independence.

triples.name: FTC independence
triples.description: the structural independence of the FTC that the EU-US adequacy decision relied on, and that Trump v. Slaughter removed.

triples.name: convergence over community
triples.description: thesis: once a format/program reaches a stable fixed point needing only maintenance, developer-community size stops being a moat, so a small EU steward can sustain it (cf. PostgreSQL, LaTeX). Subject-predicate-object triple structure is itself the candidate fixed point.

sources.name: StatCounter desktop OS Europe 2026
sources.description: StatCounter Global Stats, desktop operating-system market share Europe, Feb 2026: Windows ~69%, macOS ~18-20%, desktop Linux ~3%.

sources.name: Wikipedia usage share of OS 2026
sources.description: Usage share of operating systems, June 2026: server Linux ~63.1%; smartphone Android ~72%, iOS ~28%; desktop Linux ~3.09%.

sources.name: Synergy European cloud 2025
sources.description: Synergy Research Group, 24 July 2025: EU cloud market EUR 61bn (2024), European providers ~15%, AWS+Azure+Google ~70%, SAP and Deutsche Telekom ~2% each; cloud is 'a game of scale'.

sources.name: CNBC EU digital dependence 2026
sources.description: CNBC, 13 Feb 2026: of EU enterprise software at least 59% is US-held (Oracle 18%, Microsoft 10%); CRM dominated by Salesforce; per a Dec EU Parliament report.

sources.name: Cloudmagazin VMware 2026
sources.description: Cloudmagazin, Mar 2026: Gartner forecasts 70% of enterprise VMware customers will shift 50% of workloads to alternatives by 2028; Proxmox on ~1.5M hosts; CISPE reports 800-1500% price rises; CISPE EU complaint 19 Mar 2026.

sources.name: ETH Apertus 2025
sources.description: ETH Zurich, 2 Sep 2025: Apertus, Switzerland's first large-scale open multilingual LLM by EPFL/ETH Zurich/CSCS; architecture, weights, training data and methods fully open; 8B and 70B; Apache 2.0; trained on 15T tokens, 1000+ languages.
